critic_raw:
1. STRATIFIED RANDOM SPLIT ON FINANCIAL TIME SERIES IS THE MOST SEVERE METHODOLOGICAL ERROR POSSIBLE. Stock price data exhibits autocorrelation at multiple lags — daily, weekly, monthly — and non-stationarity (trending, regime changes). A random 70/15/15 split places observations from any given week across all three sets. The transformer trained on prices from 2023 weeks and tested on other 2023 weeks will appear to generalize when in fact it is fitting a memorized distribution. More critically, technical indicator features (lagged prices, moving averages) computed from a rolling window will directly leak future values into the past when observations are shuffled randomly. For example, if day T is in training and day T+5 is in test, the MA-5 feature for day T+5 includes the value from day T which the model has memorized. The design's own defense — 'this stratification is appropriate for detecting cross-sectional patterns' — has no application to stock price data, which has no cross-sectional units. There is only one price per instrument per day.

2. ARIMA BASELINE REQUIRES PER-STOCK TUNING BUT TRANSFORMER TRAINS ACROSS ALL STOCKS. The design specifies 'ARIMA models are tuned explicitly for each stock' while the transformer processes the full cross-sectional history. This is a structural asymmetry: ARIMA is a univariate model that cannot exploit cross-stock patterns; the transformer can learn correlations across stocks (sector effects, index co-movement). If the transformer exploits cross-stock signals and ARIMA does not, the comparison is multi-information-source vs. single-information-source, not architecture.

3. MAE ON CLOSING PRICE IS A MEANINGLESS METRIC FOR TRADING. The hypothesis claims to 'predict stock price movements' but MAE on closing prices does not measure direction accuracy. A model predicting tomorrow's closing price at exactly yesterday's close (pure persistence) achieves low MAE in trending markets while providing zero actionable signal (every prediction is wrong directionally). Directional accuracy (percent of days with correct sign of return) or Sharpe ratio of a strategy based on predictions would be meaningful; raw price MAE is not. The literature on stock prediction specifically criticizes price MAE for this reason.

4. VOLATILITY SUBGROUP ANALYSIS DOES NOT RESCUE THE MAIN EXPERIMENT. The design proposes 'separately analyzing periods of high market volatility to assess model resilience.' This is post-hoc stratification of an already invalid experiment (random split on time series). If the main experiment is leaky, the volatility subgroup analysis inherits the same leak. The analysis cannot determine whether the transformer is genuinely more robust to volatility or simply better at memorizing high-volatility price patterns from training.
all_issues_raised: Random split on financial time series directly leaks future values into lagged technical indicator features computed across shuffled observations
ARIMA per-stock tuning vs. cross-stock transformer training is a structural asymmetry conflating multi-source information with architecture
MAE on closing prices does not measure directional accuracy and cannot be used to evaluate trading signal quality
Post-hoc volatility subgroup analysis inherits the leakage from the invalid main split
Stratified random split destroys the temporal ordering that ARIMA requires; comparison between transformer and ARIMA on scrambled time series does not test the hypothesis
Lagged features computed on full series before random split create future leakage: lag-5 features for test samples may reference training-set days more recent than the test sample
MAE on absolute price levels is dominated by extreme moves and does not measure directional accuracy or trading utility
Volatility-stratified evaluation is a post-hoc sensitivity check rather than a primary evaluation design; main metric will be dominated by stable periods
Transformer attention mechanism not specified as causal vs. bidirectional; bidirectional attention on lagged features introduces architectural look-ahead bias
Random split on financial time series with adjacent-day autocorrelation is a fundamental validity error
Protocol explicitly acknowledges the split is invalid but proceeds with it anyway — the flaw is self-admitted
Transformer likely overfits to 1,250 training days; parameter count and regularization not specified
ARIMA tuned per-stock while transformer is presumably cross-stock — asymmetric tuning that may favor ARIMA
MAE on raw prices is not an actionable metric for trading; directional accuracy not reported
